text:
Bioactive peptides are short sequences of amino acids that remain embedded within the larger structure of the parent protein and remain inactive until they are released via specific actions such as enzymatic hydrolysis. Bioactive peptides have recently been in the focus of various scientific studies because they can be derived from multiple plant, animal and microbial sources. They are often produced by hydrolysis with plant derived or microbial enzymes, gastrointestinal digestion or by fermentation utilizing proteolytic starter cultures.

The unique composition and sequence of amino acids in these peptides are responsible for their wide range of activities such as antioxidant, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory and antihypertensive as well as hypocholesterolemic immunostimulant and sedative or opioid activity. The identification and purification of these bioactive peptides from protein hydrolysates require highly sophisticated techniques such as membrane separation, chromatography as well as spectroscopic and mass spectrometric methods. Bioactive peptides have tremendous potential as bioactive ingredients in food and drugs. However, their utilization in functional foods and drugs requires thorough evaluation of their stability, efficacy and safety.
